comment_content: I got off the bus at USMCRD San Diego in spring of 1969.  And the shock of that race to the yellow footprints is still embedded in my psyche--as is the indiscriminate battering witnessed from some of the more sadistic DIs that continued throughout boot camp.  Those were the days.
reply_content: What are DI’s? And why were they sadistic?

comment_content: I got off the bus at USMCRD San Diego in spring of 1969.  And the shock of that race to the yellow footprints is still embedded in my psyche--as is the indiscriminate battering witnessed from some of the more sadistic DIs that continued throughout boot camp.  Those were the days.
reply_content: @Heaveeeenxo Drill Instructors.  We had 3 rotating DI's assigned to our boot camp Platoon. Sadistic?  I'll just say a couple really seemed to relish the pain they inflicted and the power they wielded.  This was 1969, long before any of the anti-hazing and abuse policies were put in place.  So yeah, we were knocked around from time to time--and they were free to do it. No reporting system existed.  Even as late as 2019, after investigations and new regulations had long been on the books, a big USMC study showed there still remained problems with DI violence toward recruits.  There's a line between being tough and being abusively violent.  Some crossed it.

reply_content: @nighttrain1565 That is absolutely not correct. There is no law proscribing inaccuracy if there is not a collaborative agreement. In fact, the DOD encourages film and television to accurately portray the uniform, but the DOD will not give actual, official uniforms unless there is a formal Production Assistance Agreement with the DoD's Entertainment Media Liaison Office. The production is still allowed to purchase knock offs that look authentic even without collaboration, but I don't see what's dishonorable about this production.

comment_content: Excited for October 8. Boots is based on my book, The Pink Marine about my time in the USMC
reply_content:
I'm seriously looking forward to this, I served before DADT (I believe you did as well) We had gay guys all over, mostly everyone knew but NO ONE said anything. So for me it's always been this black box of what I observed and what I heard but never hearing direct from the source. Conversations on the topic back then were VERBOTTEN. I had a couple guys that said things but really didn't talk, wouldn't talk. I get it. 
 I've always had several gay friends and my short time in I probably had a few gay friends but I don't know because it was a giant closet back then. 
My understanding of this production is that you had hands on it from screen play to to screen and don't expect that you have allowed for any misrepresentation in your story so I'm going to expect some Stanley Kubrick level of boot camp shenanigans that we all went though and I'm hoping to at least get a glimpse of your perspective.  

In any case if you have somehow captured even a sliver of what it means for a human to become a Marine it will be a boon. I've struggled for almost 40 years trying to conjure images for civilians to explain this "brotherhood"  I have failed and failed miserably. I am unable to do that "becoming" justice. Ears, Open. Eyeballs, Click. How do you explain it? I'm on the edge of my seat till this airs. 

Semper Brother!
